med_suggestions.notes: Pregnancy-compatible option; a safe default when pregnancy is possible/confirmed.
med_suggestions.exclusions: Cephalosporin allergy; use caution with immediate-type severe beta-lactam allergy; adjust if renal impairment.
Immediate-type beta-lactam allergy; adjust for renal impairment.

med_suggestions.notes: Common first-line for uncomplicated cystitis when not near term and renal function adequate.
med_suggestions.exclusions: Avoid if pyelonephritis suspected (fever, flank pain); avoid if CrCl < 30 mL/min; nitrofurantoin allergy; caution near term pregnancy.

med_suggestions.notes: Single-dose alternative; generally pregnancy-compatible but confirm clinician judgment and local guidance.
med_suggestions.exclusions: Not for suspected pyelonephritis; consider availability/cost.

med_suggestions.dosing: 160/800 mg (1 DS tab) PO BID x 3 days
med_suggestions.sig: 160/800 mg (1 DS tab) PO BID x 3 days
med_suggestions.notes: Alternative when pregnancy status/trimester allows and resistance risk acceptable.
med_suggestions.exclusions: Sulfa allergy; avoid 1st trimester if possible (folate antagonist) and near term (kernicterus risk); consider local resistance; drug interactions.

note.soap:
S: 34-year-old patient. The patient is a female adult with a history of renal disease who reports experiencing dysuria for the past four days. She describes a burning sensation when urinating but denies any associated fever, flank pain, or vaginal discharge. She is not pregnant and has no known allergies except to penicillin. There are no other systemic symptoms reported.

O: Phone encounter only; unable to perform physical examination or obtain vital signs. No visual inspection possible. No urine dipstick or culture results available at this time.

A: The clinical presentation is consistent with an uncomplicated lower urinary tract infection (cystitis). Differential diagnosis includes urethritis (possibly sexually transmitted), interstitial cystitis, or irritation from other causes such as chemical exposure. Absence of fever and flank pain makes pyelonephritis less likely. Given her renal disease, careful selection of antibiotic therapy is warranted.

P: Initiate empiric antibiotic therapy appropriate for uncomplicated UTI, avoiding penicillin due to allergy and considering renal function in antibiotic choice and dosing. Recommend urine culture and sensitivity testing if feasible to guide therapy. Advise increased fluid intake and symptomatic relief measures. Phenazopyridine may be used cautiously for dysuria relief but should be avoided if any contraindications arise. Avoid NSAIDs due to underlying renal disease; acetaminophen can be used for pain if needed. Educate patient on signs of worsening infection such as fever, flank pain, or hematuria, and instruct to seek urgent care if these develop. Arrange follow-up to reassess symptoms and review culture results.
note.patient_summary: Based on what you’ve described, it sounds like you may have a bladder infection, which is common and usually treatable. Because you have a history of kidney issues and an allergy to penicillin, a licensed physician will review your information to decide if medication is appropriate and safe for you. In the meantime, drink plenty of fluids and you can use acetaminophen (Tylenol) for any discomfort. Avoid ibuprofen or similar medicines because of your kidney history. If your symptoms get worse—like if you develop a fever, pain in your side or back, or blood in your urine—please seek care the same day at an urgent care or emergency room. Since this is a phone visit, no tests or exams were done today, but a doctor will review your case and may recommend further steps. If you need an in-person exam or testing, please go to urgent care or the emergency department.
patient_instructions: If symptoms worsen or you develop new severe symptoms, seek urgent care or call 911.
Follow the disposition and safety net plan explained in the spoken summary.

Medication options you may consider if safe (no dosing by phone): acetaminophen (Tylenol) if you can take it safely (no dosing provided); ibuprofen (Advil) if you can take it safely (no dosing provided); phenazopyridine (AZO) may help urinary burning; turns urine orange; does not treat infection (no dosing provided)

Medication cautions: Avoid NSAIDs if kidney disease, stomach ulcers/bleeding history, or on blood thinners (reviewer to confirm).; Avoid phenazopyridine if pregnant; reviewer to confirm pregnancy status.
